title: KazByte: Adapting Qwen models to Kazakh via Byte-level Adapter
abstract: Large language models fragment Kazakh text into many more tokens than equivalent English text, because their tokenizers were built for high-resource languages. This tokenizer tax inflates compute, shortens the effective context window, and weakens the model's grip on Kazakh morphology. We propose to bypass the tokenizer entirely by feeding raw bytes through a small adapter that learns to speak the internal language of a frozen Qwen2.5-7B. Once the adapter is trained, we freeze it and fine-tune only the attention layers of Qwen on Kazakh text. Our central hypothesis is that this two-stage process -- first teach the interface, then adapt the model -- should match or exceed the accuracy of the original Qwen2.5-7B on standard Kazakh benchmarks. This report describes the ByteKaz architecture and training protocol. Empirical validation is ongoing; this version stakes the design and hypotheses for the record.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Recent large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable multilingual
capability (Grattafiori et al., 2024 ; Qwen Team, 2024 ) . Yet every pretrained
model is inextricably tied to a fixed tokenizer whose vocabulary is
determined before training. For lower-resource or morphologically complex
languages, this coupling creates persistent inefficiencies that neither
prompt engineering nor standard fine-tuning can fully resolve.

sections.heading: 3.1The BPE Tokenizer Tax on Agglutinative Languages
sections.text:
Let w w be a word and BPE ​ ( w ) \mathrm{BPE}(w) its tokenization. Define the token fertility as f ​ ( w ) = | BPE ​ ( w ) | f(w)=|\mathrm{BPE}(w)| . For English, 𝔼 ​ [ f ​ ( w ) ] ≈ 1.3 \mathbb{E}[f(w)]\approx 1.3 ; for Kazakh under the Qwen2.5 tokenizer, 𝔼 ​ [ f ​ ( w ) ] \mathbb{E}[f(w)] is typically much higher—a large disparity in sequence
length for the same text. Longer sequences increase training and inference
cost; exact factors depend on implementation (e.g. FlashAttention, KV cache).

sections.heading: 3.2Byte Latent Transformer
sections.text:
Pagnoni et al. ( 2025 ) introduce the Byte Latent Transformer ( BLT ), which
replaces fixed-vocabulary tokenization with a dynamic, learnable byte-to-patch
mapping. Given a byte sequence 𝐱 = ( x 1 , … , x n ) \mathbf{x}=(x_{1},\ldots,x_{n}) , a small
byte-level language model estimates the next-byte entropy H ​ ( x i ) = − ∑ v ∈ { 0 , … , 255 } p e ​ ( x i = v ∣ x < i ) ​ log ⁡ p e ​ ( x i = v ∣ x < i ) . H(x_{i})=-\sum_{v\in\{0,\ldots,255\}}p_{e}(x_{i}=v\mid x_{<i})\log p_{e}(x_{i}=v\mid x_{<i}). (1) A new patch boundary is created whenever H ​ ( x i ) > θ g H(x_{i})>\theta_{g} for a global
threshold θ g \theta_{g} . Low-entropy byte spans (predictable suffixes, common
word endings) are merged into long patches; high-entropy positions (e.g. word onsets, rare characters) receive dedicated patches.

The BLT architecture comprises three modules: 1. Local Encoder : a lightweight transformer over the byte
sequence that produces patch representations via cross-attention. 2. Global Latent Transformer : a large transformer over the
patch sequence—the primary locus of computation. 3. Local Decoder : a lightweight transformer that predicts
the next byte from patch context via cross-attention.

sections.heading: 4.1Overview
sections.text:
ByteKaz replaces Qwen2.5-7B’s embedding table and LM head with learned byte
interfaces. During Stage A the transformer body is frozen; Stage B unfreezes
attention weights only (see Section 5 ).
Figure 1 and Table 1 summarise the
components.

sections.heading: 4.2Local Encoder
sections.text:
The local encoder is a causal transformer f ϕ f_{\phi} with L ℓ = 6 L_{\ell}=6 – 8 8 layers and hidden dimension d ℓ = 512 d_{\ell}=512 . It ingests the raw byte sequence ( x 1 , … , x n ) (x_{1},\ldots,x_{n}) —with bytes embedded via a learned table E b ∈ ℝ 256 × d ℓ E_{b}\in\mathbb{R}^{256\times d_{\ell}} —and produces per-byte hidden states.
Patch representations are then obtained via cross-attention over the byte
hidden states, with one query vector per patch boundary: 𝐩 j = CrossAttn ​ ( 𝐪 j , { h i : x i ∈ patch j } ) , j = 1 , … , m , \mathbf{p}_{j}=\mathrm{CrossAttn}\!\left(\mathbf{q}_{j},\;\{h_{i}:x_{i}\in\mathrm{patch}_{j}\}\right),\quad j=1,\ldots,m, (2) where 𝐪 j ∈ ℝ d ℓ \mathbf{q}_{j}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{\ell}} is a learned query for patch j j and m ≪ n m\ll n is the (dynamic) number of patches.

Patch boundaries are determined by the entropy criterion of
Equation ( 1 ), computed by a separate small byte LM
( ≈ \approx 10M parameters) pre-trained on the training corpus.

sections.heading: 4.3Projection Layers
sections.text:
Two linear projections bridge the encoder/decoder space and Qwen’s space: 𝐩 ~ j \displaystyle\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{j} = W enc ​ 𝐩 j + 𝐛 enc , W enc ∈ ℝ 4096 × 512 , \displaystyle=W_{\mathrm{enc}}\,\mathbf{p}_{j}+\mathbf{b}_{\mathrm{enc}},\quad W_{\mathrm{enc}}\in\mathbb{R}^{4096\times 512}, (3) 𝐩 ^ j \displaystyle\hat{\mathbf{p}}_{j} = W dec ​ 𝐡 j + 𝐛 dec , W dec ∈ ℝ 512 × 4096 . \displaystyle=W_{\mathrm{dec}}\,\mathbf{h}_{j}+\mathbf{b}_{\mathrm{dec}},\quad W_{\mathrm{dec}}\in\mathbb{R}^{512\times 4096}. (4) Both projections are followed by LayerNorm. To avoid cold-start instability
(Section 8 ), W enc W_{\mathrm{enc}} is initialised so that 𝐩 ~ j \tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{j} matches Qwen’s embedding statistics: W enc ∼ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , σ emb 2 d ℓ ) , W_{\mathrm{enc}}\sim\mathcal{N}\!\left(0,\;\frac{\sigma_{\mathrm{emb}}^{2}}{d_{\ell}}\right), (5) where σ emb 2 \sigma_{\mathrm{emb}}^{2} is the empirical variance of Qwen’s embedding
matrix.

sections.heading: 4.4Qwen2.5-7B Body
sections.text:
Qwen2.5-7B’s transformer stack receives the projected patch sequence ( 𝐩 ~ 1 , … , 𝐩 ~ m ) (\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{1},\ldots,\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_{m}) with a standard
causal attention mask. We remove the embedding table and LM head entirely;
only the attention and feed-forward layers are invoked. Rotary position
embeddings (RoPE) use patch indices as position values, which is valid
because RoPE is index-based rather than fixed-length.

sections.heading: 4.5Local Decoder
sections.text:
The local decoder is a causal transformer g ψ g_{\psi} symmetric to the encoder
( L ℓ = 6 L_{\ell}=6 – 8 8 layers, d ℓ = 512 d_{\ell}=512 ). For each patch j j , it autore-
gressively predicts the bytes of patch j \mathrm{patch}_{j} conditioned on the patch
context 𝐩 ^ j \hat{\mathbf{p}}_{j} from Equation ( 4 ) and all
previously generated bytes via cross-attention: p ​ ( x i ∣ x < i , 𝐩 ^ 1 : j ) = softmax ​ ( g ψ ​ ( x < i , 𝐩 ^ 1 : j ) i ⋅ E b ⊤ ) . p(x_{i}\mid x_{<i},\;\hat{\mathbf{p}}_{1:j})=\mathrm{softmax}\!\left(g_{\psi}\!\left(x_{<i},\;\hat{\mathbf{p}}_{1:j}\right)_{i}\cdot E_{b}^{\top}\right). (6) Causal masking ensures that bytes in patch j j attend only to Qwen context
from patches < j <j and to preceding bytes within the same patch.

sections.heading: 4.6Analogy to LLaVA
sections.text:
ByteKaz is structurally analogous to LLaVA (Liu et al., 2024 ) : both
use a small modality-specific encoder, a linear projection, and a frozen LLM
body. Table 2 highlights the key differences.

sections.heading: 5Training Protocol
sections.text: We adopt a curriculum: Stage A (adapter alignment, Qwen frozen), Stage B (adapter frozen, attention-only tuning on Kazakh), optional Stage C (task SFT).

sections.heading: 5.1Stage A: Representation Alignment (adapter trained, Qwen frozen)
sections.text:
Objective. Train encoder, decoder, and projections; keep all Qwen2.5-7B
parameters frozen.

Data. English and Chinese text (1–2B tokens) so the frozen body sees
inputs near its pretraining distribution.

Loss. Primary term: byte-level cross-entropy through the adapter.
Optional hidden-state alignment (well-defined across byte vs. BPE):
on aligned text pairs, minimise MSE between patch-level hidden states from the
adapter path and segment-aggregated teacher states from a frozen Qwen2.5-7B
forward pass on BPE-tokenized text, in the spirit of attention / segment
alignment in MATT (Haltiuk and Smywinski-Pohl, 2025 ) rather than KL on mismatched
token vs. byte vocabularies: ℒ = ℒ CE ​ ( x ) + α ​ ∑ ℓ ∈ 𝒮 ‖ h patch ( ℓ ) − stopgrad ⁡ ( h teacher ( ℓ ) ) ‖ 2 2 . \mathcal{L}=\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CE}}(x)+\alpha\sum_{\ell\in\mathcal{S}}\bigl\|h^{(\ell)}_{\mathrm{patch}}-\operatorname{stopgrad}\bigl(h^{(\ell)}_{\mathrm{teacher}}\bigr)\bigr\|_{2}^{2}. (7) Here h patch ( ℓ ) h^{(\ell)}_{\mathrm{patch}} are hidden states at selected layers 𝒮 \mathcal{S} along the patch path; h teacher ( ℓ ) h^{(\ell)}_{\mathrm{teacher}} are
teacher states after pooling to patch boundaries; sg \mathrm{sg} is stop-gradient.
Setting α = 0 \alpha{=}0 is a valid baseline (CE only).

Success signal. BPB on held-out English/Chinese in a reasonable range;
optional alignment loss decreasing.

sections.heading: 5.2Stage B: Kazakh Adaptation (adapter frozen, attention-only in Qwen)
sections.text:
Objective. Freeze the adapter; train only attention weights in Qwen2.5-7B (e.g. W Q , W K , W V , W O W_{Q},W_{K},W_{V},W_{O} per layer; optionally input LayerNorm
before attention). MLP / FFN blocks remain frozen. Rationale:
attention routes information between positions; the new patch geometry may
primarily require reweighting dependencies, while FFN stores much factual
capacity we wish to preserve.

Data. Kazakh text from the SozKZ pretraining corpus (Tukenov, 2026c , b ) : 9B tokens collected from 18 public
sources including CulturaX, HPLT 2.0, mC4, MADLAD-400, CC-100, Kazakh
Wikipedia, and others, cleaned via a 9-stage pipeline (48.2% pass rate from
28.4M raw documents). Available on Hugging Face as saken-tukenov/sozkz-corpus-clean-v3 . Target: 5–10B tokens where
feasible; see also open corpora in Section 7 .

Patch-size calibration. Ablate entropy threshold θ g \theta_{g} ; report
average patch size and BPB.

sections.heading: 5.3Stage C: Task Fine-tuning (optional)
sections.text:
Objective. Instruction following or benchmark-aligned formats (e.g. MC QA, reading comprehension). Adapter can remain frozen; continue
attention-only updates or add a small task head. Data: Kazakh instruction
sets, 50–200M tokens typical.

sections.heading: 6.1Intrinsic Metrics
sections.text:
• Bits-per-byte (BPB) on held-out Kazakh text: primary
language modelling quality metric. • Average patch size : measures compression efficiency;
higher is better. • Alignment loss (optional MSE in Equation ( 7 )):
how well patch-path hidden states match the teacher.

sections.heading: 6.2Downstream Benchmarks
sections.text:
We evaluate on the same three Kazakh benchmarks as Tukenov ( 2026c ) (Sect. 4.1 of arXiv:2603.20854). The following subsections follow that
paper’s breakdown; Table 4 summarises the suite.

sections.heading: 6.3Evaluation protocol
sections.text:
To match the reproducibility style of Tukenov ( 2026c ) (Sect. 4.3):

• Logit-based / likelihood scoring. Tasks are scored without
text generation where possible, reducing sensitivity to decoding
hyperparameters. • Multiple-choice tasks. Each candidate answer is scored by the
sum of its token log-probabilities conditioned on the prompt,
normalised by token count (full answer likelihood). • Zero-shot. No in-context exemplars and no task-specific
fine-tuning before benchmark scoring, unless an ablation explicitly
targets supervised adaptation. • Reporting. Compare ByteKaz checkpoints (Stage A/B) to
the baselines listed in Section 6.4 ; primary reference
is Qwen2.5-7B + BPE and SozKZ-600M (Tukenov, 2026c ) .

sections.heading: 6.5Ablations
sections.text:
1. Patching strategy: entropy vs. fixed-stride vs. whitespace. 2. Encoder size: d ℓ ∈ { 512 , 768 , 1024 } d_{\ell}\in\{512,768,1024\} . 3. Alignment weight: α ∈ { 0 , 0.1 , 0.5 , 1.0 } \alpha\in\{0,0.1,0.5,1.0\} in
Equation ( 7 ). 4. Attention-only vs. attention + LayerNorm vs. last- k k full layers
(if needed) in Stage B. 5. Script: Cyrillic Kazakh vs. Latin Kazakh; cosine distance between
paired representations.

sections.heading: 7Open Kazakh Text Corpora on Hugging Face
sections.text:
We list openly accessible dataset repos on Hugging Face that include
a Kazakh ( kk ) subset or Kazakh-specific configuration, with
verification notes from the dataset cards (as of the writing of this note). Gated datasets require login / acceptance; they are not fully open
without that step.

sections.heading: 9Conclusion
sections.text:
We have proposed ByteKaz , a byte-level adapter around Qwen2.5-7B with BLT -style local encoder and decoder. The central hypothesis is staged training : align the adapter with Qwen frozen (Stage A), then
freeze the adapter and tune only attention layers on Kazakh (Stage B),
with optional task SFT (Stage C). We hypothesise Kazakh accuracy can match or exceed the baseline Qwen2.5-7B + BPE tokenizer on the same
tasks—an empirical question.

We summarised failure modes, evaluation against Qwen2.5-7B + BPE, and a
verified list of open Kazakh corpora on Hugging Face
(Section 7 ). This note stakes the architecture and
protocol for collaborators; experiments are future work.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.text:
Untested at scale. This is a research proposal; no empirical
results are presented. All efficiency and quality estimates are projections
based on BLT and LLaVA analogues.

Alignment objective. Equation ( 7 ) uses hidden-state MSE
optional; aligning teacher/student segments requires careful string alignment
implementation.

Entropy model. The byte LM for patch boundaries may be biased
toward high-resource languages; Kazakh-specific entropy models are optional.

Qwen Kazakh exposure. Stage B (attention-only on Kazakh) is intended
to close the gap; if insufficient, selective FFN unfreezing is a fallback
ablation.

figures.caption:
Figure 1: The ByteKaz architecture. Blue modules are trained in Stage A.
The Qwen2.5-7B stack is frozen during adapter training; in Stage B the
adapter is frozen and attention sublayers in Qwen are updated on Kazakh data.
Bytes are grouped into entropy-based patches, projected into Qwen’s space, and
processed by the transformer; the local decoder predicts bytes autoregressively.
